coredata.dc:title: “Vigna radiata” based green C-dots: Photo-triggered theranostics, fluorescent sensor for extracellular and intracellular iron (III) and multicolor live cell imaging probe
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Navpreet
Sharma, Vinay
Tiwari, Pranav
Saini, Anoop K.
Mobin, Shaikh M.
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent C-dots have drawn increasing attention due to their superior properties such as high aqueous solubility, optical absorptivity, favorable biocompatibility and appreciable photoluminescence. Nature offers various precursors for the synthesis of green C-dots like fruits, vegetables, flowers, animal derivatives and many more. Herein, nitrogen doped C-dots (N@VRCD) were developed by economical, one step hydrothermal method using “Vigna radiata” sprouts as sole carbon precursor and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source, which was optimized for enhanced optical properties. To the best of our knowledge, N@VRCD shows excellent quantum yield ∼58%. Importantly, N@VRCD also exhibits significant light induced phototoxicity to cancerous cells which enable the possibility of using it as a potential theranostics agent. Further, the N@VRCD displays highly sensitive and selective sensing behaviour towards Fe3+ ions by getting fluorescence “turn-off” with the limit of detection as low as 140 nM. Moreover, N@VRCD shows biocompatible and hemocompatible nature with a potential of multicolor live cell imaging and intracellular Fe3+ detection which was validated by confocal microscopy and flow cytometry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green
C-dots
Photodynamic therapy
Intracellular Fe3+ sensing
Singlet oxygen
Quantum yield
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fe (III) ions detection and selectivity studies
Cytotoxicity, bio-imaging and flow cytometric studies
Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation assay
Singlet oxygen (1O2) measurements by DPBF chemical trapping
Evaluation of cellular response towards N@VRCD
The mechanism for fluorescence quenching of N@VRCD
Selectivity and sensitivity studies of N@VRCD towards Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for synthesis strategy of N@VRCD.
Structural characterization of N@VRCD (a) PXRD (b) FT-IR (c) XPS survey spectra. (d–f) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s respectively (g) TEM micrograph (h) HR-TEM (i) SAED pattern.

Optical characterization of N@VRCD (a) (i) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N@VRCD (ii) Fluorescence excitation spectra, (iii) emission spectra of N@VRCD. (Right inset) colour of N@VRCD solution without & with UV irradiation (360 nm), (b) excitation dependent emission (c) fluorescence lifetime decay (d–f) Stability of N@VRCD: at varying UV illumination (d), at different temperature (e) and at the varying time (f).
Bio-imaging of HeLa cells using N@VRCD (50μg mL−1) at 37℃ for 2 h at various excitation wavelengths.

Hemocompatibility of N@VRCD (A) graphical representation of % hemolysis after 1 h incubation of RBCs with different concentration of N@VRCD (B) Camera images of control and experimental samples. (C) SEM images of N@VRCD treated RBCs.

(a) Cell viability of A375 and HeLa after 24 h with various concentrations of N@VRCD (50μg mL−1–1000 μg mL−1) (b) Cell viability of A375 and HeLa cells after 1 h exposure to visible light (400–700 nm).
(a) The absorbance decay spectra of DPBF in the presence of N@VRCD with various irradiations (b) Relationship between Absorbance of DPBF with irradiation time in the presence of N@VRCD.

The study of shift in fluorescence intensity of only cells (control) compared with the addition of different additives under different conditions as mentioned in A375 cells shown as a histogram, done using flow cytometer.
(a) N@VRCD’s Fluorescence spectra after addition of Fe3+ (concentration range from 100μM-3000μM) (b) Relationship between concentration of Fe3+ and F/F0.
The mechanism for fluorescence quenching of N@VRCD.

Selectivity in the sensing response of various metal ions (c = 1.0 × 10−2 M) with N@VRCD (0.5 mg mL−1 in water) (inset) Fluorescent recovery after quenching with Fe3+ upon addition of various cations and anions.

Intracellular sensing of Fe3+ in HeLa cells treated with N@VRCD (50μg mL−1) at 37℃ for 2 h with the help of confocal microscopy (a) Bright field (b) at λex/ λem = 405/450 ± 25 nm (c) N@VRCD+ 500μM of Fe3+ (d) Intracellular fluorescence intensity determination of control, N@VRCD treated and N@VRCD + Fe3+ treated HeLa cells using flow cytometry.
The comparison of C-dots prepared by using “Vigna radiata” with other recently reported green precursor derived C-dots.

head.ce:title: “Vigna radiata” based green C-dots: Photo-triggered theranostics, fluorescent sensor for extracellular and intracellular iron (III) and multicolor live cell imaging probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Discipline of Biosciences and Bio-Medical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Simrol, Khandwa Road, Indore 453552, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Discipline of Biosciences and Bio-Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Discipline of Metallurgy Engineering and Materials Science, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Simrol, Khandwa Road, Indore 453552, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Discipline of Metallurgy Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Discipline of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Simrol, Khandwa Road, Indore 453552, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Delhi, Delhi 110 007, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Discipline of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Simrol, Khandwa Road, Indore- 453552, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: “Vigna radiata” based green c-dots for photo dynamic therapy (PDT), multicolor imaging and sensing of Fe3+.

Fluorescent C-dots have drawn increasing attention due to their superior properties such as high aqueous solubility, optical absorptivity, favorable biocompatibility and appreciable photoluminescence. Nature offers various precursors for the synthesis of green C-dots like fruits, vegetables, flowers, animal derivatives and many more. Herein, nitrogen doped C-dots (N@VRCD) were developed by economical, one step hydrothermal method using “Vigna radiata” sprouts as sole carbon precursor and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source, which was optimized for enhanced optical properties. To the best of our knowledge, N@VRCD shows excellent quantum yield ∼58%. Importantly, N@VRCD also exhibits significant light induced phototoxicity to cancerous cells which enable the possibility of using it as a potential theranostics agent. Further, the N@VRCD displays highly sensitive and selective sensing behaviour towards Fe3+ ions by getting fluorescence “turn-off” with the limit of detection as low as 140 nM. Moreover, N@VRCD shows biocompatible and hemocompatible nature with a potential of multicolor live cell imaging and intracellular Fe3+ detection which was validated by confocal microscopy and flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:para: Plants and their natural products have been used profusely since ages. Use of green source for the synthesis of C-dots is gaining attention due to its easy availability and increased environmental concerns, moreover green synthesis of nanoparticles is cheap as well as efficient. Being a fresh member of nanomaterial family, fluorescent C-dots have drawn much attention because it accentuates excitation dependent multi-color emission, substantial biocompatibility and hemocompatibility, stable photoluminescence, resistance to photo-bleaching and environmental affability [1–7]. However, maximum times C-dots synthesized from green source come up with low quantum yield (QY) [8–15]. Various green sources were explored for the synthesis of carbon dots like coffee beans, grass, orange juice, peanut shells, cabbage and banana juice having QY 3.8%, 6.2%, 26%. 9.91%, 16.5% and 8.95% respectively [9,13,16–18] (Table S1) [19]. Apart from using various edible sources, biomasses like lignin were used as carbon source for synthesizing carbon dots [20,21]. High quantum yield is the very much desirable factor for any fluorescent material to be used as imaging agent [22]. Hence there is huge requirement to develop low cost, green, high yielding method and favourable source for preparation of C-dots. The C-dots have a huge impact on advancement in the field of intracellular imaging such as in human cell lines, fungal cells etc. [23–25]. The nitrogen doping leads to enhancement of QY due to similar atomic size and presence of five free valence electrons to be introduced in carbon [26–28]. Therefore, Nitrogen is the commonly used doping element for QY enhancement [29–32].

An efficient approach to treat cancer is the demand of today’s world [33]. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is upcoming therapeutic modality used for cancer treatment [34]. It is a fruitful and distinct method of killing malignant cells in a more manageable way [35–38] Due to its minimal invasiveness, it is gaining interest widely [39]. There is an enormous demand of light activated therapeutic agents for PDT. The major cause associated with the effectiveness of PDT was the generation of Reactive oxygen species (ROS) [40]. When C-dots are irradiated, it leads to electron transfer to H2O generating free radicals thus killing nearby cells [41,42]. Semiconductor quantum dots were used in PDT but their cytotoxic nature due to the presence of heavy metal ions limit their use in biological systems. The other limitations of PDT agents are hydrophobicity, low ROS generation and photobleaching [43]. Further people have used C-dots surface passivated with the photosensitizer (PS) in PDT. The high 1O2 quantum yield of a material is another favorable factor for using it as a PS agent [40,44].So there is urgent need to develop PDT agent showing good biocompatibility, photostability with adequate ROS generation.

Besides the use of C-dots as bioimaging agent and photosensitizer, the other captivating application is metal ion sensing [45]. Transition metals like Fe3+ play important role in the chemistry of living organisms. Ample amount of iron is present in biological systems, which play important role in many of the metabolic activities inside the body like oxygen transportation, gaseous exchange and is part of many enzymatic reactions [46]. It is the central component of hemoglobin and integral part of many proteins. Many diseases like Anaemia, sickle cell anaemia and iron overload, hemochromasia, Alzheimer's etc. happen due to decreased and elevated level of Fe3+ respectively. Because of the hydrophobic nature of many available Fe3+ sensors, they are non-soluble in water which hinders their use in biological systems [47].

In this work, the nitrogen doped C-dots (N@VRCD) were synthesized by one pot hydrothermal method using a sustainable resource i.e. “Vigna radiata” sprouts which serve as abundant sources of carbon for the preparation of N@VRCD and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source for doping. N@VRCD exhibits phototoxic behavior in the presence of visible light, which could be further explored for developing a new environment friendly photosensitizer agent. Besides, it shows “turn-off” response towards Fe3+ ions which is further studied intracellularly using confocal microscopy and flow cytometry. A green chemistry of synthesis as well as use of as prepared c-dots in photo-triggered theranostics using “vigna radiata” has not been reported to date to the best of our knowledge. So, herein we present, for the first time, the synthesis of fluorescent c-dots using vigna radiata as sole carbon source and their evaluation for biocompatibility, live cell imaging, photothermal therapy and intracellular as well as extracellular Fe3+ ion sensing.

The purchasing of “Vigna radiata” (green gram) seeds was done from local market. Ethylenediamine was attained from sigma-Aldrich. The anions and metal salts were procured from sigma-Aldrich and Merck. No further purification was done of the received analytical grade chemicals. The whole work was completed using Sartorius milli-Q system’s Deionized water (DI).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was analyzed using a Rigaku, RINT 2500V X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (1.5406 Å). The Varian Cary 100 Bio UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to perform UV–vis spectroscopic studies. Fluoromax spectrofluorometer was used to conduct fluorescence studies. IR spectra (4000–400 cm−1) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were recorded using Bio-Rad FTS 3000MX instrument and AXIS ULTRA respectively. TEM images were recorded using a FEI Tecnai G2- F20 Transmission Electron Microscope. The cellular imaging studies were carried out by using an Olympus laser scanning microscope. Synergy H1 Biotek microplate reader used for absorption studies of MTT assay. Flow cytometric analysis was performed on BD LSR Fortessa.

Hydrothermal carbonization was used for the synthesis of N@VRCD. “Vigna radiata” seeds (250 g) were washed thoroughly with running tap water and were immersed in 500 mL of RO water for 12 h. Soaked seeds were kept covered with a damp cloth for 24 h to get sprouts. The sprouts were ground using domestic mixer by adding 200 mL of RO water to get a thick “Vigna radiata” extract. Further 10 mL of “Vigna radiata” extract was mixed with different concentrations from 200 μL to 1000 μL of ethylenediamine. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min to achieve a homogenous solution. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon lined autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for the period of 24 h. The autoclave was kept at room temperature for cooling down. In order to purify, the product suspension was centrifuged at high speed to remove large particles. Black sticky product (N@VRCD) was obtained by lyophilisation and was re-suspended in water as per requirement.

The quantum yield was calculated according to the procedure suggested in the manual of "A Guide to Recording Fluorescence Quantum Yields" by HORIBA Jobin Yvon IBH Ltd. and also to the published procedure [48]. The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) of N@VRCD was calculated by using Eq. (1) where quinine sulphate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 was taken as the standard (Φst = 0.54)
(1) ΦF = Φst × Ss / Sst × Ast /As × n2 s / n2 st
where ΦF is the emission quantum yield of the sample, Φst is the emission quantum yield of the standard i.e quinine sulphate (0.54) [49], Ast and As represent the absorbance of the standard and the sample at the excitation wavelength, respectively, while Sst and Ss are the slope from the plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity vs. the absorbance of the standard and the sample at different concentration respectively, and nst and ns are the solvent refractive index of the standard and the sample, and subscripts s and st refer to the sample and the standard, respectively. The detailed procedure is described in supplementary Method 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe (III) ions detection and selectivity studies
ce:section.ce:para: The metal ions detection procedure was as follows.

The fluorescence spectrum of 2 mL aqueous solution of N@VRCD was recorded at λex. 360 nm and maximum intensity were set as control (F0). In the meantime, all the metal solutions were prepared by dissolving salts of Na+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, and K+ in milli-Q water to get a final concentration of 10 mM. The solution of different metal ion as-synthesized was mixed with the N@VRCD solution and fluorescence spectra were recorded at λex. 360 nm. All the experiments were done at room temperature under similar conditions. The highly sensed metal ion was determined and further sensing experiments for the same were carried out at lower concentration (0–1000 μM). Subsequently, competitive selectivity for Fe3+ was done in the presence of other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, bio-imaging and flow cytometric studies
ce:section.ce:para:
Cell viability was studied using HeLa (cervical cancer cell line) and A375 (human melanoma cell line) by performing 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT assay) [50,51]. The purple colored formazan produced by reduction of yellow tetrazolium by metabolically active cells were quantified by dissolving them in DMSO and recording absorbance at 570 nm. Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium supplemented with 1% antibiotics penicillin/streptomycin, 10,000 U mL−1 and 10% (v/v) foetal bovine serum was used to grow cells in 96-well plate. 1 × 104 cells in 100 μL of growth media were seeded in each well and allowed to grow using 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 ℃ for 24 h. Different concentrations of N@VRCD ranging from 50 μg mL−1 to 1000μg mL−1 were added to the wells in triplicates. The wells containing growth media only were taken as control. After 24 h incubation, the N@VRCD containing media was replaced with fresh media having MTT (0.5 mg mL−1) dissolved in it, which was further incubated for 4 h. The purple formazan crystals so formed were dissolved in 100 μL of DMSO. The absorbance was read at 570 nm and percent cell viability was calculated using following Eq. (2).
(2) % Cell Viability = Abs (Exp . )/ Abs (Control) × 100

Bio-imaging was done using HeLa and A375 cells grown in confocal dishes for 24 h and treated with N@VRCD (250 μg mL−1) for 2 h. The cells were washed with 1X PBS twice after treatment and live cell images were taken using confocal laser scanning microscope using blue, red and green laser sources.

The Flow cytometric studies were carried out to investigate further the staining abilities of N@VRCD and its sensing behavior towards Fe3+ ions. Cells were grown in 6-well plate until it achieves 100% confluency. Cells of one well without treatment were taken as control. The stained sample consists of cells treated with N@VRCD (250 μg mL−1) for 2 h. For Fe3+ ion sensing the cells first treated with N@VRCD and then exposed to the Fe3+ solution. The cells were trypsinized and pallet was collected after centrifugation which was further dissolved in 1X PBS for flow cytometric analysis.

Photo-toxicity studies were performed in a similar manner as that of cytotoxicity studies. Two 96 well plates were divided into two groups: dark control and light experiment group. Cells were grown for 24 h. Treatment of N@VRCD was given from 50 μg mL−1 to 1000 μg mL−1 in both the plates. After 4 h of treatment, cells of 96 well plate belonging to light experiment group was exposed to the visible light lamp (400–700 nm) for 1 h and again incubated for next 20 h. The other dark control 96 well plate was also kept under identical conditions but without irradiation. Cell viability was measured by MTT assay as mentioned above and %cell viability was calculated subsequently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation assay
ce:section.ce:para: 2′, 7′- dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCF-DA) was used to determine the generation of Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) in live cells. The ROS generation studies were carried out using HeLa as well as A375 cell lines (plated in duplicate). After 24 h of growth, the cells were incubated in the presence and absence of N@VRCD (treatment concentration = IC50 value in presence of light) dispersed in DMEM and incubated for 4 h. Further, one set was photo-irradiated and other kept in dark. DCF-DA (30μM) was added, and the plates were incubated in the dark at 37 °C for 30 min. Cells without DCF-DA were treated as negative control. All the cells were trypsinized, centrifuged, suspended in 1X PBS and analyzed in the flow cytometer using λexc. = 488 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Singlet oxygen (1O2) measurements by DPBF chemical trapping
ce:section.ce:para: The singlet oxygen (1O2) generation capability of N@VRCD was assessed by using 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a detection probe [52]. DPBF act as 1O2 scavenger [53]. For irradiation experiment, a visible light (400–700 nm) was used. N@VRCD mixed with DPBF (3 × 10−5 M) and irradiated. The decrease in absorbance value of DPBF at 414 nm was observed spectrophotometrically. The singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ) was calculated by using Methylene blue (MB) (ΦΔ=0.57 in dichloromethane) as standard [54].

The hemolytic activity of N@VRCD was investigated further to check its hemocompatibility. Fresh blood was collected from a healthy human in sodium citrate vial. The blood was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min and supernatant plasma was discarded. The collected RBC’s were washed three times with 1X PBS and treated with N@VRCD in the concentration range of 0.01 mg mL−1 to 50 mg mL−1. Subsequently, the tubes were incubated for 60 min at 37 °C and centrifuged at 700 × g for 10 min. The amount of free hemoglobin in the supernatant was determined by measuring the absorbance of the supernatant at 540 nm in the microplate reader. Sterile saline solution and Triton X 100 (0.2%) were used to set negative and positive controls, respectively [3] All the experiments were run in triplicates. To investigate any change in the morphology of RBC’s, SEM was done after fixing cells in 4% glutaraldehyde followed by dehydration with 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%, and 100% ethanol serially. Institute Human Ethics Committee (IHEC), IIT Indore was consulted for permission to use a human blood sample.

The nitrogen doped N@VRCD was synthesized using the hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 24 h (Scheme 1 ). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was used as nitrogen source for doping. The reaction conditions were optimized to obtain maximum quantum yield, by using different concentrations range from 200 to 1000 μL of ethylenediamine per 10 mL of “Vigna radiata” extract and time range from 5 h to 36 h (Method S1). The highest quantum yield was found to be ˜58%. The increase in quantum yield can easily be explained on the basis of nitrogen doping [55]. Only “Vigna radiata” extract was also used for the synthesis of C-dots (VRCD) without any doping agent and results were summarised in Figs. S1 and S2. With addition of EDA the relative fluorescence intensity gets increased drastically with more than 80%. Further, more clear difference can be seen in fluorescence intensity of VRCD and N@VRCD at emission wavelength-422 nm as shown in Fig. S1b. Thus, with nitrogen doping fluorescence emission gets enhanced of N@VRCD w.r.to VRCD.

The X-ray diffraction pattern showed one broad peak centred at 20.9° with the interlayer spacing of 4.2Å which is greater than graphite (3.3 Å) attributed to distorted sp2 carbon structure (Fig. 1 a). The surface ligands had been analysed using FT-IR, which shows prominent absorbance peaks of functional groups at 3428 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 which includes stretching mode of CH, OH and NH, along with the vibrational mode of CO at 1664 cm−1 and bending vibrations of CH2 at 1401 cm−1 with an epoxide peak at 1048 cm−1 (Fig. 1b) [3,13].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out for further analysis of structural information. The survey spectrum of N@VRCD shown in Fig. 1c shows three peaks with binding energies of 284.8 eV, 399.2 eV, and 531.2 eV, reveals the presence of graphitic or aliphatic carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s) and oxygen (O1s) respectively [11]. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s 3 peaks of binding energies 284.7 eV, 285.8 eV and 287.9 eV were obtained after deconvolution, which attributes to CC/CC (graphitic or aromatic carbon), CN and CO (epoxy and alkoxy), and CO species respectively [11,56] (Fig. 1d). The deconvoluted spectrum of N1s exhibited N atom of both pyridinic type (399.7 eV) and pyrrolic type (400.4 eV) which shows successful doping of C-dots with nitrogen (Fig. 1e). The high-resolution O1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into two peaks of energies 531 eV and 532.2 eV which exhibit the presence of CO and NO bonds respectively Fig. 1f [57–59].

Further, the morphology of N@VRCD was explained by TEM (Fig. 1g). It exhibits that the shape is mostly spherical and size of C-dots is less than 10 nm. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 1h) displays clear lattice fringes which indicate crystallinity of N@VRCD [60]. SAED pattern shown in Fig. 1i also confirms the poor crystallinity of N@VRCD. The presence of crystalline, as well as amorphous particles, is similar to previous reports of C-dots [60].

The aqueous solution of N@VRCD shows two distinctive absorbance peaks at 276 and 344 nm (Fig. 2 a (i)). The former peak attributes to π−π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain and later n-π* transition of CO [61]. The absorbance peaks in the longer wavelength region are often seen after surface passivation, as we cannot observe peak in the longer wavelength region in the absorbance plot of VRCD Fig. S1c [62]. Concurrently, Fig. 2a (inset) shows visual changes in the light yellow coloured solution of N@VRCD to a bright blue fluorescence when irradiated with UV trans-illuminator at 365 nm.

Excitation dependent fluorescence spectra were measured and shown in (Fig. 2a (ii) & (iii)). Emission maxima which is centred at 422 nm was obtained at 360 nm excitation wavelength, along with the excitation-dependent emission wavelength which is the evident feature of C-dots (Fig. 2b) [63]. Emission maxima ranging from about 410 nm to 530 nm could be utilized in multicolour imaging [64]. Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) (Fig. 2c) was performed to obtain insight about the fluorescence response’s kinetics of N@VRCD using a 375 nm diode laser. The data obtained after best three-exponential function fitting showed the average life time of N@VRCD was around 2.67 ns. Such a short average life time indicates that the fluorescence in N@CRCD was because of radiative recombination nature [65,66].

Further, the fluorescence stability was determined under UV irradiation, by the varying temperatures, time and pH. N@VRCD shows exceptional stability after UV irradiation as depicted in Fig. 2d. Sample exposed to 125 W UV (365 nm) lamp for 120 min retains 88% of initial fluorescence intensity. Similarly, fluorescence was stable at different temperatures up to 82% when kept at −80 °C, −20 °C, 0 °C, 25 °C, 37 °C and 90 °C for 2 h (Fig. 2e). Even after about 160 days of synthesis, about 88% of initial fluorescence intensity was retained (Fig. 2f). The fluorescence ability of N@VRCD was relatively stable in pH range 2–10 (Fig. S3). Thus N@VRCD found to be highly stable under harsh conditions, which could be suitable for bio-imaging as well as intracellular sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cellular response towards N@VRCD

ce:section.ce:para: Along with optical superiority excellent biocompatibility is needed for N@VRCD for its biomedical applications. The cell viability studies were performed on HeLa cells and A375 cells (a human cervical cancer cell line and melanoma cell line respectively) to determine the biocompatibility of N@VRCD. The results shown in Fig. 5a illustrates more than 75% cells viability after treatment with high concentration ranging from 50μg mL−1 to 1000μg mL−1 for 24 h.

Further, when Hela cells and A375 cells, were excited with 405 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm lasers after 2 h incubation with N@VRCD, it showed blue, green and red colour fluorescence respectively (Fig. 3 ). The staining pattern confirms the membrane permeability of N@VRCD and it also perpetuates its multicolor emission property in the cellular environment. However, the accurate localization could not be interpreted because of N@VRCD emission spread over a wide range.

To check the human blood compatibility, hemolysis ratio test was performed. Due to health implications of C-dots, it’s important to understand the effect of C-dots on red blood cells (RBC). The results obtained from hemolysis study showed in Fig. 4
a ratify hemocompatible nature of N@VRCD. Less than 5% hemolysis was observed even when RBC’s were exposed to very high concentration of 50 mg mL−1. Positive control clearly shows the red colour of the supernatant due to the presence of free hemoglobin, while the treated samples do not show any red colour of the supernatant and their colour resemble that of negative control (Fig. 4b). Any material showing less than 5% hemolysis considered as hemocompatible [67]. The SEM images of RBC’s treated with varying concentration of N@VRCD further confirm the biconcave morphology of RBCs and support the hemocompatible nature (Fig. 4c). Thus, surface passivation supports the non-hemolytic nature of N@VRCD and were consistent with earlier reports [68].

It has already been established from bioimaging studies that N@VRCD is being taken up by the cells and is non-cytotoxic as well. However phototoxicity of carbon nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots were investigated [40] for further applicability thus, photo cytotoxicity of N@VRCD were investigated here. The photocytotoxicity profile in the presence of visible light (400–700 nm) was studied to evaluate the photodynamic capability of N@VRCD, which was calculated in terms of IC50 value in HeLa and A375 cells by using MTT assay. N@VRCD found to be surprisingly photocytotoxic with IC50 value in Hela and A375 cells having concentrations 505 μg/mL and 310μg/mL respectively (Fig. 5) when exposed to visible light while being non-cytotoxic in dark. The plausible mechanism of cytotoxicity was generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). To get further insights of mechanism ROS generation was determined by using a singlet oxygen scavenger DPBF in vitro while intracellular ROS generation was determined by using DCF-DA, ROS detection assay.

In order to foresee the possibility of intracellular ROS generation, the ability of N@VRCD to generate singlet oxygen was studied. One of the most important photochemical properties of a photosensitizer was singlet oxygen production. The singlet oxygen produced by N@VRCD upon irradiation reacts with DPBF, leads to decrease in the absorbance at 414 nm. As shown in Fig. 6 a absorbance value was taken after each irradiation with the time interval of 5 s. The significant and gradual decrease in absorbance clearly indicates the formation of photo-induced singlet oxygen in the solution of N@VRCD in DCM. At the same time when the only N@VRCD solution in DCM with DPBF was irradiated, no change in absorbance value indicates any degradation of N@VRCD in the presence of light. The singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ) was found to be 0.41. The results obtained clearly indicate the role of ROS in photo-induced cell death. A linear relationship between time and absorbance value was shown in Fig. 6b.

According to earlier reports when certain nanoparticles were irradiated with light, they get excited and react with molecular oxygen present in the system to give rise to ROS and leads to cytotoxicity. Carbon dots upon photoexcitation lead electron excitation from the valence band to the conduction band and forms electron–hole pair. However, when these electrons combines with surface bound molecules such as oxygen, free radicals are formed and leads to formation of hydroxyl radical [42].

To further confirm the intracellular generation of ROS in the presence of light, DCFDA assay was carried out, which was widely used to measure the cell’s redox state. 2′-7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFDA) a non-fluorescent compound which can easily enter the cell via cell membrane and gets accumulated inside the cell which on oxidation by ROS species provides a highly fluorescent compound 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). The concentration of DCF could be measured by exciting it by 485 nm which gives emission maxima at 530 nm.

The studies were carried out on A375 cell line. The samples taken were only cells (control), only DCFDA treated cells and cells treated with DCFDA as well as N@VRCD after irradiation for 1 h with visible light of 400–700 nm or in dark (as control). A clear shift was easily observed in the fluorescence data obtained using FACS, of cells treated with N@VRCD then exposed to light as compared to the cells treated with N@VRCD alone or with N@VRCD and DCFDA in dark (Fig. 7 ). This infers the formation of ROS by N@VRCD on visible light exposure however no such observation was observed when placed in dark, which supports lower cell viability due to ROS arbitrated cell death. To the best of our knowledge present work is the first report investigating the photo-toxic nature of green carbon dots on human cell lines.

The dual ability of C-dots to act as an electron acceptor as well as the electron donor leads to fluorescence quenching due to the electron transfer between metal and the C-dots. The water- soluble nature of N@VRCD makes it a potential candidate to be used as the sensor in biological systems.

Fluorescence “turn off” response of water-soluble N@VRCD towards Fe3+ was one of the interesting findings and was consistent with earlier report [69]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence quenching was observed when the aqueous solution of Fe3+ in the range of 0–3000 μM was added to the N@VRCD solution. A good linear correlation was found in the range of 100–1000 μM and 1000–2000 μM for which an adj. R2 value is 0.98 and 0.99 respectively (Fig. 8b). The following equation was used to calculate limit of detection [51].
(3) Limit of detection (LOD) = 3.3(σ/S)
In this equation S represents the calibration curve’s slope and σ represents standard error. LOD was found to be as low as 140 nM. Further, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased to 13% of the initial intensity of N@VRCD after addition of 3000 μM Fe3+ solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for fluorescence quenching of N@VRCD
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous reports, the “turn-off” behaviour of N@VRD can be explained by electron transfer mechanism and formation of the metal-fluorophore complex [8,28,70]. As elaborated in Fig. 9 plentiful phenolic hydroxyl group (OH) present on the surface of N@VRCD form specific coordination interaction with Fe3+. Formation of this complex leads to splitting of d orbital of Fe3+, which facilitates the non-radiative electron-hole recombination, leading to signiﬁcant ﬂuorescence quenching. The Quencher molecule (Fe3+ in this case) reacts with the fluorophore (N@VRCD) either at ground state or at excited state leads to static or dynamic quenching [71]. Apart from this the redox potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ is in the middle of the conduction and valence bands of N@VRCD, so photo-induced electrons can transferred to the complexed Fe3+ from N@VRCD’s surface, resulting in fluorescence quenching [72,73]. To confirm the electronic communication between N@VRCD system and Fe(III), theoretical calculations were performed on the basis of density functional calculation. Two types of basic sets were used in DFT calculations, (i) 6-31G basic set were used for carbon (C), hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O) and Nitrogen (N) and (ii) LanL2DZ basic was used for Fe (III). The energy and geometry optimization were performed using Gaussian 09 programme [74,75]. HOMO and LUMO energy of N@VRCD system was found to be −4.2936 eV and −2.9124 eV respectively. The LUMO energy of N@VRCD was found to higher than that of the standard reduction potential of Fe2+/Fe3+ system which is 0.77 V, which clearly indicate the favorable electron transfer from N@VRCD to Fe(III). Moreover, the HOMO and LUMO energy of N@VRCD system was calculated which was found to be −4.2936 eV and −2.9124 eV respectively. While in case of N@VRCD –Fe(III) HOMO and LUMO energy was found to be −4.5970 eV and −3.2977 eV respectively. Moreover, The theoretical band gaps of N@VRCD and N@VRCD –Fe(III) was found to be 1.38 and 1.29 eV respectively (Fig. S5).

Further to get insights of fluorescence response kinetics of N@VRCD, TCSPC was done using a 375 nm diode laser with and without addition of Fe3+ in N@VRCD solution in water. The results obtained were shown in Fig. S5 revealed that there is not much difference in average life lime of the control sample i.e. N@VRCD alone and after addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ solution indicating the static quenching in N@VRCD-Fe3+ system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity studies of N@VRCD towards Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, to evaluate the selectivity of the sensor specifically towards the Fe3+, the fluorescence quenching effect of various metal ions and anions (Co2+, Li+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, V+, Mo+, Sn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ga3+, Fe2+, Na2+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, La3+, Pt+, Hg2+, Ni2+, S2−, CH3COO-, CO3 2−, No3-, PO4 3-, S2O8 2− and SO4 2−) on N@VRCD was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 10 among all the metal ions, Fe3+ exhibits the greatest effect on fluorescence quenching. Moreover, to check the effect of other anions and cations on the sensing behaviour of N@VRCD, competitive selectivity studies were performed. As shown in Fig. S6, even in the presence of various other anions and cations the N@VRCD was able to sense Fe3+ effectively. The quenched fluorescence of N@VRCD/Fe3+ was recovered up to 32% of original when Phosphate ions were added to the mixture. No other anion or cation showed any such effect as shown in Fig. 10(inset). This could be due to competitive interaction of N@VRCDs between phosphate molecules and Fe3+.

The suitability of N@VRCD as intracellular Fe3+ sensing agent was checked, using confocal microscopy and flow cytometry. The fluorescence was quenched completely when cells treated with N@VRCD were incubated with Fe3+ (Fig. 11 a–c), which verify the authenticity of spectroscopic studies. The experiment performed using flow cytometry also endorse fluorescence quenching after addition of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 11d). It must be mentioned here that N@VRCDs acts as a nanosensor for sensing of Fe3+ having excellent sensing capability with better limit of detection and broader liner range w.r.to other reported literatures (Table S3). Also the green synthetic approach, very high quantum yield, excellent bio and hemo compatibility makes N@VRCD a potential candidate for its application in Fe3+ sensing The multifunctionality of "Vigna radiata" derived c-dots is compared with the c-dots synthesized using other green precursors and is summarized in Table 1 .

A new green source “Vigna radiata” was explored for easy synthesis of multi-talented C-dots which were doped with nitrogen for getting very high quantum yield (∼58%). The nitrogen doped surface passivated C-dots (N@VRCD) exhibited excellent water solubility and bright multicolor fluorescence with great photo-stability. The as-prepared green C-dots were photo-toxic due to the formation of ROS in the presence of light and were clearly observed by singlet oxygen measurement and intracellular ROS detection studies. The novelty of the present work opens grounds to explore N@VRCD for making a new chemotherapeutic agent for cancer treatment. Moreover, it showed highly selective and sensitive “turn-off” response towards Fe3+ ions. The reasonable biocompatibility and hemocompatibility makes it a propitious multicolor fluorescent agent for live cell imaging, biolabeling, and intracellular Fe3+ ion sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Sophisticated Instrumentation Centre (SIC), IIT Indore for all the characterization facilities. N.K. and V.S. would like to thank UGC, New Delhi for fellowship and AKS would like to thank UGC for Kothari fellowship. P.T. gratefully thanks MHRD for fellowship. S. M. M. thanks DST-SERB (Project: EMR/2016/00113), and CSIR {Project: 01 (2935)/18/EMR-II}, New Delhi, Govt. of India for a research grant and IIT Indore for financial support. We gratefully acknowledged the service of MNCF, IISc Bangalore for providing XPS facility, SAIF- IITB for TEM and FTIR facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.04.039.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
